name: Badb Extended
description: Badb, meaning 'crow' or 'raven', is a complex and powerful figure in Irish mythology, primarily associated with war, fate, and prophecy. Often considered one of the Morrígan, a trio or group of war goddesses, Badb's influence extends beyond mere battlefield frenzy. She is a shapeshifter, frequently appearing as a crow or raven, and her presence is an omen of impending death or doom. Badb's screams and cries are said to incite terror and confusion amongst enemy ranks, ensuring victory for her chosen side. She is also linked to the Tuatha Dé Danann, the supernatural race who inhabited Ireland before the arrival of the Gaels. Her prophecies are often cryptic and veiled, requiring interpretation to understand their true meaning. Beyond her role in war, Badb possesses a deep connection to the land and its cycles, embodying the destructive and regenerative forces of nature. She is a reminder of the unpredictable and often brutal realities of life and death, a potent symbol of both destruction and renewal. Some interpretations also link her to sovereignty and the right to rule, suggesting a more nuanced role than simply a harbinger of death. Her appearance can also signify the end of an era, the collapse of old structures, and the beginning of something new, making her a figure of both fear and hope.
subtitle: War, Fate, and Prophecy Goddess

keyMyths.title: Badb's Prophecy
keyMyths.description: Before the Battle of Mag Tuired, Badb prophesied the downfall of the Fir Bolg, foretelling their defeat and the rise of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Her words were a grim warning of the coming conflict and its devastating consequences.

keyMyths.title: The Battle Cry
keyMyths.description: During battles, Badb would shriek and cry, causing confusion and terror among the enemy. This supernatural battle cry was a powerful weapon, ensuring victory for the Tuatha Dé Danann.

keyMyths.title: Shape-shifting into a Crow
keyMyths.description: Badb often took the form of a crow or raven, appearing on battlefields to observe the carnage and influence the outcome. Her presence as a bird was a clear sign of impending death and doom.

sources.author: Patricia Monaghan
